coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen and sulfur doped carbon nanomaterials based on cucurbiturils for visual detection of nitroaniline
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Li
Cen, Ran
Xiao, Zhiyou
Tao, Zhu
Xiao, Xin
coredata.dc:description: Nitroaniline (NA), specifically p-Nitroaniline (p-NA), is a toxic and poorly biodegradable compound that poses significant risks to the environment and human health. Therefore, the development of an efficient method for detecting p-NA is paramount. In this study, blue fluorescent carbon dots, Q[6]-CDs and Q[7]-CDs, were synthesized using cucurbit[n]uril (Q[n]) and mercaptosuccinic acid (MSA), exhibiting remarkable abilities to differentiate NA isomers and visually detect p-NA. By leveraging the internal filter effect (IFE) and electron transfer (ET) quenching mechanism, Q[7]-CDs achieved rapid and sensitive detection of p-NA with a limit of detection (LOD) as low as 0.61 µM. Furthermore, a supramolecular fluorescent array sensor utilizing these CDs successfully discriminated between NA isomers. Additionally, Q[7]-CDs were integrated with a smartphone to create a portable fluorescent sensing platform for visual detection of p-NA in water samples, achieving a LOD of 1.76 µM. When applied to the detection of p-NA in tap water and river water, spiked recoveries ranged from 102.8 % to 110.0 % and 103.0 % to 110.9 %, respectively. The sensors demonstrated high sensitivity, robustness against interference, and simplicity in application, indicating their potential for use in environmental monitoring.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanomaterials
Cucurbit[n]uril
Fluorescent probe
Host-guest interaction
Nitroaniline

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Anti-interference ability experiment of coexisting ions
Structural properties of Q[6]-CDs and Q[7]-CDs
Optical properties of Q[6]-CDs and Q[7]-CDs
Mechanism of fluorescence response to p-NA
Detection of p-NA in real water

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the synthesis and response of sensor to p-NA.
(a) XPS survey spectrum, (b) C 1 s XPS spectra, (c) N 1 s XPS spectra, (d) O 1 s XPS spectra and (e) S 2p XPS spectra for Q[7]-CDs.

(a) The 3D histogram of the fluorescence intensity of Q[7]-CDs in the presence of 16 aromatic compounds. (b) The linear discriminant analysis of o-NA, m-NA and p-NA based on the fluorescence array sensor. (c) The photos of the fluorescence emission changes of Q[7]-CDs in the presence of 16 organic compounds. (d) The linear relationship of Q[7]-CDs sensing for p-NA in range of 15.7－60.01 µM.
The 1H NMR spectra of pure MSA, Q[7]-CDs (400 µg.mL−1), p-NA (5 × 10−4 M) and Q[7]-CDs@p-NA in D2O at 25 °C.

(a) A schematic diagram of the detection of p-NA using a smartphone color recognizer. (b) The fluorescence color of Q[7]-CDs changed with the concentration of p-NA. (c) The linear plot of the RGB value (B/G).
Recoveries of p-NA in Laboratory tap-water and environmental water samples.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen and sulfur doped carbon nanomaterials based on cucurbiturils for visual detection of nitroaniline

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Macrocyclic and Supramolecular Chemistry of Guizhou Province, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guizhou University, Guiyang 550025, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Macrocyclic and Supramolecular Chemistry of Guizhou Province
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aKey Laboratory of Macrocyclic and Supramolecular Chemistry of Guizhou Province, Department of Chemistry, Guizhou University, Guiyang 550025, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Guizhou Institute of Technology, Guiyang 550025, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical Engineering, Guizhou Institute of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bSchool of Chemical Engineering, Guizhou Institute of Technology, Guiyang 550025, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitroaniline (NA), specifically p-Nitroaniline (p-NA), is a toxic and poorly biodegradable compound that poses significant risks to the environment and human health. Therefore, the development of an efficient method for detecting p-NA is paramount. In this study, blue fluorescent carbon dots, Q[6]-CDs and Q[7]-CDs, were synthesized using cucurbit[n]uril (Q[n]) and mercaptosuccinic acid (MSA), exhibiting remarkable abilities to differentiate NA isomers and visually detect p-NA. By leveraging the internal filter effect (IFE) and electron transfer (ET) quenching mechanism, Q[7]-CDs achieved rapid and sensitive detection of p-NA with a limit of detection (LOD) as low as 0.61 µM. Furthermore, a supramolecular fluorescent array sensor utilizing these CDs successfully discriminated between NA isomers. Additionally, Q[7]-CDs were integrated with a smartphone to create a portable fluorescent sensing platform for visual detection of p-NA in water samples, achieving a LOD of 1.76 µM. When applied to the detection of p-NA in tap water and river water, spiked recoveries ranged from 102.8 % to 110.0 % and 103.0 % to 110.9 %, respectively. The sensors demonstrated high sensitivity, robustness against interference, and simplicity in application, indicating their potential for use in environmental monitoring.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon Dots (CDs) represent a cutting-edge category of 0-dimensional, carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials, and these particles, typically less than 20 nm in size, are composed of sp2/sp3 hybridized carbon skeletons, supplemented by a rich array of functional groups or polymer chains [1,2]. The homogeneity of surface functional groups on carbon dots often results in a lack of specific active sites. Consequently, surface modification can effectively adjust the surface state of carbon dots, altering their optical and physical properties, and thereby broadening their application fields [3]. Chitosan, a natural polymeric material, has garnered significant attention due to its unique infrared spectroscopic properties and excellent biocompatibility. Furthermore, the enhancement of nanomaterial stability through microencapsulation technology renders chitosan a promising candidate as a surface modification material for carbon dots [4,5]. CDs are considered promising alternatives to semiconductor quantum dots and have found broad applications in biomolecular detection and recognition, disease diagnosis and detection, drug delivery, biological imaging, and catalysis [6–9]. It has even been reported that “quantum dot” nanostructures have been applied to the pharmaceutical and cosmetic industries [10].

Supramolecular macrocycles including cyclodextrins, cucurbit[n]uril, pillararene etc. [11]. Besides, Wang and his colleagues reported a dynamic porous framework system of macrocyclic trimers for information encryption [12]. Cyclodextrins possess a hydrophilic exterior, making them soluble in aqueous media, and a hydrophobic cavity that can host guest molecules of different sizes [13]. Cucurbit[n]uril, also known as Q[n] or CB[n], is a type of macrocyclic molecule, distinguished by its rigid structure, a hydrophobic cavity, and two portals made up of n carbonyl groups [14–17]. Q[n] is a class of macrocyclic compounds characterised by having open-ended rigid cavities consisting of C=O groups capable of forming stable host-guest inclusion complexes with small molecules through their negatively charged portals and electroneutral cavities, thus exhibiting properties different from those of the original Q[n] and the small molecules in which it is encapsulated [18]. The port C=O, which can act as a hydrogen bond acceptor, and the hydrophobic cavity contribute to the enhanced binding affinity between cucurbiturils and guest molecules, while improving the binding specificity [19]. Furthermore, these unique structural properties endow this supramolecular macrocyclic compound with multi-functionality in various fields, including analytical detection, biosensing, as well as adsorption and separation [20–25].

NA is a widely used organic compound, yet p-NA poses severe toxicity and poor biodegradability, posing risks to soil and groundwater [26,27]. Hence, it is crucial to efficiently detect NA levels in wastewater to ensure human safety, environmental protection, and public health and its associated health effects include damage to the spleen, kidney and liver [28]. So far, the commonly used detection methods include high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [29], luminescent analytical methods [30,31], and electrochemical methods [32]. Consequently, a simple analytical method to detect p-NA needs to be developed.

It is well known that Q[n] exhibits low solubility in both aqueous and organic solvents. Therefore, we adopted a strategy to react with small molecules rich in hydrophilic groups under high temperature and high pressure, and finally obtained carbon nanomaterials conforming to the typical characteristics of carbon dots. This approach greatly improves the solubility of the resulting materials, while also conferring excellent luminescent properties and stability. Herein, Blue fluorescent carbon dots (Q[6]-CDs, Q[7]-CDs), possessing high stability and good solubility, were synthesized using Q[n] and MSA, and subsequently employed for the specific recognition of p-NA (Scheme 1 ). The precursors underwent dehydration, polymerization, carbonization, and passivation processes to form CDs. The carbon dots formed from small molecular precursors containing amino and carboxyl groups exhibited high quantum yields and good hydrophilicity [33]. Utilizing the IFE and ET quenching mechanism, Q[7]-CDs was employed as a fluorescent sensing probe for p-NA, achieving a LOD as low as 0.61 µM. In addition, supramolecular fluorescence array sensors were constructed using two kind of CDs, enabling the differentiation of NA isomers. Finally, a portable visual fluorescence sensing platform was proposed to establish a simple method for rapid detection of the organic pollutant p-NA in aqueous environments with a low LOD of 1.76 µM.

Cucurbit[6]uril and Cucurbit[7]uril were synthesized according to the published procedure [34]. o-Nitrophenol (o-NP), m-Nitrophenol (m-NP), p-Nitrophenol (p-NP), p-Nitrotoluene (p-NT), o-Nitrobenzaldehyde (o-NBD), 8-Hydroxyquinoline (8-HQ), p-Phenylenediamine (p-PD), Catechol (CA) and p-Nitrobenzoic acid (p-NB) were purchased from Shanghai McLean Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. o-Nitroaniline (o-NA), m-Nitroaniline (m-NA), Bisphenol A (BPA), o-Phenylenediamine (o-PD) and m-Phenylenediamine (m-PD) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent (Shanghai) Co., Ltd.as analytical pure standards. p-Nitroaniline (p-NA, analytically pure standard) was purchased from Tianjin Komio Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.; mercaptosuccinic acid (MSA, 98 %) was purchased from Shanghai Yien Chemical Technology Co., Ltd. NaCl, KCl, MgSO4, ZnSO4, Bi(NO3)3, D2O and CaCO3 were purchased from Aladdin Reagent (Shanghai) Co., Ltd. as analytical pure standard. All the above reagents were not further treated before use, and the solvents used were ultrapure water (conductivity was 18.25 MΩ•cm).

The fluorescence spectra were recorded on Varian Cary Eclipse spectrofluorometer(Varian, Inc., Palo Alto, CA, USA). UV–visible spectra were recorded on an Agilent 8453 spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, Santa Clara, CA, USA). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on Bruker Vertex 70. 1H NMR spectra were recorded on JEOLJNM-ECZ400 s spectrometer. The structure of the carbon dots was also investigated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, USA, Thermo fisher Nexsa). X-ray diffractometry (XRD) analyses were performed on Thermo Scientific Smart Lab. The morphology of the carbon dots was observed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Germany, ZEISS Gemini SEM 300) and the particle size distribution of the carbon dots was measured by dynamic light scattering (DLS, BI-200SM Wide-angle Dynamic Static Light Scattering), and the quantum yields were measured using an Edinburgh FS5 fluorescence spectrometer. Automatic Surface Area and Porosity Analyzer (BET, Canta AUTOSORB IQ)

The synthesis of Q[n]-CDs was achieved through a hydrothermal process utilizing cucurbiturils and MSA as starting materials. Specifically, MSA was reacted separately with 160 mg of Q[6] and 100 mg of Q[7], dissolved in 60 mL of water. The solution was sonicated to ensure homogeneity and then heated in a PTFE-lined reactor at 180 °C for 12 h. After cooling, the mixture underwent filtration, centrifugation, and dialysis for purification. Finally, rotary evaporation and drying yielded powdered Q[6]-CDs and Q[7]-CDs.

2.5 mL of Q[n]-CDs solution was added to a cuvette, followed by the addition of 200 µL of the detected substance, (initially at a concentration of 1 × 10−3 M, resulting in a final concentration of 74 µM. The mixture was allowed to react for two minutes before fluorescence spectra were scanned for the mixed solution of Q[6]-CDs and Q[7]-CDs with the detected substances.

To investigate the specificity and sensitivity of two types of carbon dots, Q[6]-CDs and Q[7]-CDs, in detecting distinct isomers of NA. Add 200 µL of o-NA, m-NA and p-NA to 4 mL Q[6]-CDs and Q[7]-CDs with a concentration of 400 µg.mL−1 (the starting concentration of NA is 1 × 10−3 M, resulting in a final concentration of 8 × 10−5 M). Subsequently, fluorescence spectra were recorded after a two-minute incubation period. The recorded fluorescence intensity data were then collated into a data matrix consisting of 2 sensor elements × 3 contaminants × 5 replicates.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anti-interference ability experiment of coexisting ions
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the ionic immunity of the sensor, 200 µL of p-NA at a concentration of 1 × 10−3 Mwas added to the Q[7]-CDs and the Q[7]-CDs@p-NA fluorescence intensity was measured, and then equal amounts of K +, Na+, Mg2+, NO3 −, Sr2+, Zn2+, Cl−, SO4 2−, CO3 2− were added to the Q[7]-CDs@p-NA solution and the fluorescence intensity was re-evaluated under the same conditions. The test conditions were the same as the experiments described above. This was aimed at determining whether common ions significantly altered the Q[7]-CDs@p-NA fluorescence response-response, thereby affecting the accuracy and reliability of the assay.

A simple sensing platform consisting of a smartphone and a box equipped with a 365 nm UV lamp was constructed to use the Q[7]-CDs detection of p-NA. Different volumes of p-NA solution (0, 25, 50 … 325, 350 µL) were added dropwise to 2.5 mL of Q[7]-CDs solution, and the mixed solution was placed in a cuvette, which was put into the UV lamp box, and the fluorescence image was captured by the smartphone. Then, the color recognition color picker application installed on the smartphone was used to pick up the color of the captured fluorescence image and output the corresponding RGB values.

The feasibility of Q[7]-CDs for detecting p-NA in both tap water and river water has been verified through a series of spiked recovery experiments. The tap water was collected from the laboratory at Guizhou University, whereas the river water originated from the Shilihetan Watershed in Huaxi District, Guizhou Province. The actual water samples underwent filtration through a 0.22 µm nylon membrane to remove suspended particles, and the resulting clarified solutions were collected for subsequent experimental analysis. Standard solutions of p-NA were subsequently added to these pre-treated samples, yielding spiked concentrations of p-NA at 11.98 µM, 35.87 µM and 47.77 µM, respectively. Following this, the fluorescence emission spectra of the spiked samples were recorded for evaluation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural properties of Q[6]-CDs and Q[7]-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of Q[n]-CDs as depicted in Fig. S1, reveal that both types of carbon dots are spherical in shape. Furthermore, dynamic light scattering measurements (DLS) indicate that the average particle size distributions of two CDs are very similar, 5.79 nm and 5.80 nm respectively (Fig. S2). The surface structural properties were verified by testing the specific surface areas and pore size distributions of two carbon dots, labeled as Q[6]-CDs and Q[7]-CDs, which exhibited BET surface areas (Fig. S3) of 0.0239 m2.g-1 and 0.1671 m2.g-1, separately. The pore diameter distributions were presented in Fig. S4. The adsorption isotherms indicate that the N2 adsorption capacity is low at low pressures, suggesting a relatively weak interaction between Q[n]-CDs and N2, which results in poor adsorption performance of the material [35]. According to the XRD patterns (Fig. S5), the positions of sharp peaks exhibited by the two types of carbon dots are quite similar, with Q[6]-CDs and Q[7]-CDs centered at 20.6° and 21.4°, respectively, corresponding to the graphite (002) [36].

The interaction between Q[n] and MSA was investigated by 1H NMR measurements. Figs. S6 and S7 show that the H1, H2, H3 proton signal peaks of MSA shifted to the up-field upon the addition of Q[6], while the H4 signal peaks shifted to the down-field. This implies that the methylene and hypomethylene groups of MSA entered the rigid hydrophobic cavities of Q[6], while H4 was located at the port of Q[6]. Furthermore, Figs. S6 and S8 illustrate that the signal peaks of H1, H2 and H3 of MSA shifted to the high field after the reaction of Q[7] with MSA. The proton peaks of H1 and H2 of MSA shifted from 2.93 ppm and 2.90 ppm to 2.56 ppm, respectively, and H3 shifted from 3.75 ppm to 3.40 ppm. This indicates that the methylene and hypromethyl sites of MSA entered the cavity of Q[7]. Furthermore, the 1H NMR spectra of two CDs differed slightly due to the larger cavity of Q[7]. Q[n] has been successfully introduced into carbon dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of Q[6]-CDs and Q[7]-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties of Q[n]-CDs are investigated by the QYs, UV absorption spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy. The QYs of Q [6]-CDs and Q [7]-CDs are shown in Fig. S9, which are 1.77 ％ and 3.88 ％, respectively. Fig. S10 shows the fluorescence emission spectra, optimal excitation and optimal emission wavelength of Q[6]-CDs. Fig. S11 shows the optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of 365 nm and 460 nm for Q[7]-CDs separately. In the ultraviolet-visible spectroscopy of the Q[n]-CDs (Figs. S10b, S11b), the absorption peaks between 300 nm and 350 nm are due to the n-π* jump of conjugated C=O in the structure of Q[n]-CDs [37].

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was performed of Q[n]-CDs. As shown in Fig. S12, Q[6]-CDs and Q[7]-CDs exhibited similar infrared spectra. The peak at 3492.96 cm−1 was assigned to the stretching vibration of -OH; the peak at 2345.35 cm−1 corresponded to the typical stretching vibration of -SH [38,39]; the characteristic peaks at 1732.01 cm−1, 1386.76 cm−1 and 1109.02 cm−1 were assigned to the C=O, C-N and C-O stretching vibrations, respectively; and the peak at 1323.11 cm−1 was assigned to the C-S stretching vibration.

XPS characterization was performed to determine the bonding environment and surface chemical state of the Q[n]-CDs. The XPS survey spectrum (Fig. 1a ) showed that the Q[7]-CDs consisted of C, N, O and S elements with atomic percentages of 41.09 %, 18.45 %, 22.93 % and 3.92 %, respectively. The high-resolution XPS spectrum (Fig. 1b) of C 1 s was deconvoluted into three peaks at 284.6 eV, 287.2 eV, and 288.8 eV, which corresponded to C-C/C=C, C-N/C-O/C-S and C=O bonds, respectively. The high-resolution XPS spectrum (Fig. 1c) of N 1 s was observed at 399.6 eV, corresponding to C-N-C. The high-resolution XPS spectrum (Fig. 1d) of O 1 s was deconvoluted into two peaks at 531.3 eV and 532.7 eV, corresponding to C=O and C-OH, respectively. The high-resolution spectra (Fig. 1e) with peaks at 163.2 eV and 164.5 eV corresponded to S2p3/2 and S2p1/2 spin-orbit couplings, a photoemission energy that was usually associated with -SH in XPS databases [39]; whereas, the peak at 168.6 eV was assigned to the sulphur oxide SOx [40]. The peaks of the XPS spectra of the Q[6]-CDs (Figs. S13 and S14) had similar XPS spectral peaks with Q[7]-CDs. In conclusion, the XPS and FTIR results indicated that the Q[n]-CDs were successfully doped with nitrogen and sulfur atoms, and the surfaces of the prepared Q[n]-CDs were successfully modified by functional groups such as C=O, COOH, C-N and -SH.

Since the cavity size of Q[7] can accommodate a wide range of small aromatic or ring-structured organic molecules [41], sixteen structurally similar aromatic compounds that may be present in contaminated water were selected for a series of identification detection experiments, and the structure is shown in Fig. S15. As shown in Fig. S16, Q[6]-CDs exhibited a certain degree of quenching effect on o-NP, p-NP, o-NA and p-NA. However, Q[7]-CDs only showed a more obvious quenching efficiency on o-NA, p-NA (Fig. 2a ), and the degree of quenching followed the relationship of p-NA>o-NA>m-NA. The quenching efficiency of o-NA was 30 %, while the p-NA quenching efficiency reached 76 %, did not cause much interference in the detection of p-NA. The changes of Q[7]-CDs under UV lamp after the addition of different pollutants are shown in Fig. 2c. Therefore, two fluorescent probes were successfully used to construct a simple supramolecular fluorescence sensing array to discriminate the isomers of NA (Fig. 2b).

Due to the highly sensitive selectivity of Q[7]-CDs to p-NA, the effect of different concentrations of p-NA on the fluorescence intensity of Q[7]-CDs was further explored. The fluorescence emission spectra of Q[7]-CDs upon dropwise addition of different p-NA concentrations are shown in Fig. S17. And in Fig. S18, nonlinear fitting curves for changes in the fluorescence intensity of the inclusion complex in the presence of different concentrations of the p-NA. The quenching efficiency reached 94 % when the concentration increased from 0 µM to 149.6 µM. Moreover, a good linear relationship between p-NA and the fluorescence intensity of Q[7]-CDs was observed in the concentration range of 15.7∼60.1 µM. The linear equation was y = 4.227x+61.542, R2 = 0.994, which was consistent with the Lineweaver-Burk equation (Fig. 2d). Eventually, the LOD was calculated as 0.61 µM.

The above experimental results demonstrate that Q[7]-CDs can serve as a detection sensor for p-NA. To evaluate the anti-interference ability of this sensor in complex environments, the effect of various ions on the detection of p-NA was further investigated. As shown in Fig. S19, when an equal amount of metal ions (K +, Na+, Mg2+, NO3 −, Sr2+, Zn2+, Cl−, SO4 2−, CO3 2−, Bi3+) was added to the Q[7]-CDs solution containing p-NA, the fluorescence intensity of the mixture changed very slightly. Therefore, it can be concluded that the sensor has good anti-interference performance in a complex environment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of fluorescence response to p-NA
ce:section.ce:para: To elucidate the quenching mechanism, As shown in Fig. S20a, a partial spectral overlap between the UV absorption spectrum of p-NA and the emission and excitation spectra of Q[7]-CDs was observed, indicating that an IFE contributed to the quenching of Q[7]-CDs [42]. Different concentrations of p-NA were gradually added to the Q[7]-CDs and the UV absorption spectra were recorded, as shown in Fig. S20b. The UV absorption spectra of Q[7]-CDs@p-NA did not change with the increase of p-NA concentration, suggesting that no chemical change occurred in the system to form the host-guest complex, and that an ET effect might be involved.

To further examine whether an ET quenching mechanism was present in the system, Q[7]-CDs@o-NA, Q[7]-CDs@m-NA and Q[7]-CDs@p-NA were analyzed by nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy. The comparison of the 1H NMR spectra (Figs. S21 and S22) of free o-NA and Q[7]-CDs@o-NA showed that the signals of Ha, Hb, Hc and Hd proton peaks of o-NA shifted to the high-field upon the incorporation of o-NA into Q[7]-CDs, indicating that o-NA entered the cavity of Q[7]. In contrast, for the Q[7]-CDs@m-NA system, the addition of m-NA to Q[7]-CDs (Figs. S23 and S24) caused only a small displacement of the proton peak Hc on the benzene ring to the high field, while the rest of the proton peaks of Ha, Hb and Hd shifted to the low field, suggesting that m-NA was located at the port of Q[7]. For the Q[7]-CDs@p-NA system (Figs. S25, 3 ), the addition of p-NA resulted in a high-field shift of Ha and a low-field shift of Hb, implying that the part of the p-NA molecule attached to the -NO2 group entered the hydrophobic cavity of Q[7], while the part attached to -NH2 was at the port of Q[7]. The chemical shifts of MSA in the three systems were also compared. It was found that the H1 and H2 proton resonance peaks of MSA were shifted to the lower field after the addition of NA. In contrast, the H3 and H4 peaks were unchanged, indicating that the NA did not completely displace MSA from the Q[7] cavity after the NA entered the cavity of Q[7].

Due to its polar carbonyl port and hydrophobic cavity, Q[n] can form stable host-guest complexes with small molecules such as protonated aliphatic and aromatic amines [43]. Moreover, most of the guest molecules that can bind strongly to Q[n] (n = 7 and 8) are cationic molecules, as they can interact with the carbonyl linkage port of the host molecule through cation-dipole interactions [44]. In this work, the presence of MSA at the port (-SH group of MSA) hindered the NA from entering the Q[7] cavity completely, while 1H NMR spectra revealed that the -NO2 end of p-NA had penetrated the Q[7] cavity, and that the quenching effect of p-NA on Q[7]-CDs was the most significant. The combined analysis of nuclear magnetic resonance and fluorescence spectroscopy suggested that a ET process occurred from the excited Q[7]-CDs to the guest molecules in the Q[7] cavity, which might explain the good fluorescent response of Q[7]-CDs to p-NA [45,46].

The quenching efficiency of p-NA and o-NA on Q[7]-CDs was compared and it was found that o-NA had a weaker quenching effect on Q[7]-CDs. This could be attributed to the spatial site-barrier effect that prevented the -NH2 and -NO2 parts of o-NA from entering the cavity of Q[7], thus limiting the electron transfer from Q[7]-CDs to o-NA. Similarly, m-NA could not satisfy the conditions for electron transfer with Q[7]-CDs due to the spatial site resistance effect that hindered it from entering the cavity of Q[7], and thus m-NA had almost no quenching effect on Q[7]-CDs. Since MSA had occupied part of the Q[7] cavity, the spatial site resistance effect and the nature of the substituent groups on the aromatic ring influenced the penetration of NA into the cavity of Q[7]. Among them, p-NA had the most obvious cavity-matching effect, as its strong electron-withdrawing group, the -NO2 end, entered deep into the hydrophobic cavity, while its electron-donating group, the -NH2 end, was located at the cavity outside, which was more favourable for electron transfer between Q[7]-CDs and p-NA, resulting in the most significant quenching effect of p-NA. Besides, the hydrophobic cavity of Q[6] was smaller than that of Q[7], this also explained why the quenching effect of p-NA on Q[6]-CDs was not as effective as that on Q[7]-CDs.

A simple sensing platform consisting of a smartphone and a box equipped with a 365 nm UV lamp was constructed to use the Q[7]-CDs detection of p-NA as a low-cost, rapid and portable on-site visual detection strategy [47]. The color recognition color picker application was used to pick up the color of the captured fluorescence image and output the corresponding RGB values (Fig. 4a ). The variation of fluorescence color of Q[7]-CDs@p-NA mixed solution with p-NA concentration is shown in Fig. 4b. The linear relationship between the ratio value of the blue channel to the green channel and the concentration of p-NA (Fig. 4c) was studied, and a standard curve showing a good linear relationship between p-NA and the (B/G) of Q[7]-CDs in the concentration range of 19.6∼65.4 µM (y = 0.0404x+1.2579, R2 = 0.998) was obtained. The LOD of the visual detection was 1.76 µM by the calculation. The integration of smartphones with carbon dot sensing platforms leverages advanced image processing to optimize colorimetric detection signals, thereby enhancing accuracy and stability. This cost-effective, swift, and portable system enables real-time, location-independent monitoring, bypassing the need for laboratory facilities. Consequently, the significance of carbon dot platforms for rapid, on-site environmental pollutant analysis is emphasized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of p-NA in real water
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the sensor's applicability for identifying p-NA in real samples, tap water from Guizhou University and river water from Huaxi River were selected for p-NA spiking recovery experiments (Table 1 ). p-NA was not detected in tap-water and river water. Different concentrations of p-NA standard solutions were added to the two samples and measured using the experimental method (n = 4). The recovery of p-NA in tap water ranged from 102.8 % to 110.0 %, with an RSD between 2.49 % and 3.15 %. The recovery of p-NA in river water ranged from 103.0 % to 110.9 % (2.25 %≤RSD≤2.80 %), indicating that the Q[7]-CDs prepared in this study have potential as a fluorescent probe for rapidly detecting p-NA in the environment.

A comparison of several reported methods for the determination of p-NA in different samples is shown in Table S1. Our method improves the sensitivity of the analysis method, and the LOD is an order of magnitude higher than the common fluorescence method in the literature. This work also enables the construction of a simple supramolecular fluorescence sensing array for the differentiation of the isomers of NA. Moreover, Q[7]-CDs can be used to visualize and quantify p-NA via a smartphone sensing platform with a limit of detection of up to 1.76 µM.

In summary, our research has culminated in the successful synthesis of two types of blue fluorescent CDs with exceptional water solubility. Notably, the inherent spatial compatibility of the Q[n] cavity with benzene-ring-containing small molecules renders these cucurbituril-based CDs ideal candidates as fluorescent sensors for p-NA. As corroborated by 1H NMR analysis, the unique substitution patterns on the benzene rings of nitroaniline (NA) isomers dictate their distinct insertion modalities into the Q[n] cavity, thereby modulating the extent of charge transfer interactions with the CDs. Consequently, the quenching efficiency of Q[n]-CDs exhibits variability among the three NA isomers. Capitalizing on both IFE and ET quenching mechanisms, a supramolecular fluorescent array sensor was devised, capable of selectively detecting p-NA and discriminating among NA isomers. Notably, upon exposure to p-NA, Q[7]-CDs undergo a pronounced decrease in fluorescence intensity, as evident from fluorescence spectroscopic measurements. This phenomenon enables the rapid detection of p-NA with a remarkable detection limit of 0.61 µM. Moreover, to facilitate on-site and accessible analysis, a portable sensing platform was developed that integrates CD sensors with smartphones and a complementary color recognition application. This innovative system enables both visual and quantitative detection of p-NA, achieving a detection limit of 1.76 µM. Our findings hold a certain potential for advancing the field of carbon dot-based sensing platforms and their diverse applications in chemical analysis, environmental monitoring, and beyond.

Li Liu: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation. Ran Cen: Methodology, Conceptualization. Zhiyou Xiao: Supervision, Software. Zhu Tao: Writing – review & editing. Xin Xiao: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Science and Technology Foundation of Guizhou Province (Qiankehe Jichu ZK[2021]077).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.molstruc.2024.140504.
